Title: Deep Work: Rules for Focused Success in a Distracted World
Review:
It took me a while to write down a review for this book. In fact, I'd started reading another one before I decided to gather my thoughts and put them into words.

I picked up this book to hopefully solve my continuous problem of procrastination, particularly whenever I try to study my Math majors. To troubleshoot my inability to focus in complex tasks, I was hoping Cal could provide me details on the walls that prevent my studying from becoming smooth sailing. Unfortunately, this book did not solve my Math procrastination problem — but it gave me equally, if not more, significant insights that would help tremendousy in other aspects of my life. 

The key theme of this book is that focus is a skill and should be treated more carefully than money. You should be carefully on where and what you are paying attention to because attention is a limited resource. As such, when time spent on trivial matters is accumulated, it becomes detrimental to the progress you could have otherwise achieved.

I've began applying the core lessons of this book over the past few days by being mindful of things that fracture my attention. I've also stopped multi-tasking and greatly reduced dopamine machines by avoiding short contents like the plague. Throughout this experiment, I've discovered that Netflix doesn't fracture nor reduce the quality of my attention so I shall be keeping it even after classes start. This was a very unexpected book that severed my habit of doom scrolling.

It is also this book that helped me realize that my ideal pomodoro length is 90 minutes instead of the usual 25 because (1) it takes a while to ease into focus, (2) I don't like being interrupted once I get into the task. 

Deep Work resonated with me on a personal level. I’ve often been described as a deep person, so this book felt like it was shedding light on a part of me I hadn’t fully explored. I thought, “If I can think deeply, then I should naturally be able to focus deeply.” The insights I gained from this book will certainly influence the choices I make going forward, especially in my career.

Title: Harry Potter and the Goblet of Fire
Moods: adventurous, dark, emotional, funny, hopeful, reflective, sad, tense
Review:
The densest book of the series so far with the thickest plot and a lot of interesting new characters. As always, the Defence Against the Dark Arts professor is integral to the plot. I absolutely loved the world-building J.K. Rowling employed (especially the dragons). Almost cried when Harry and Ron made up. Ron was 90% the reason why every book was enjoyable, and I'm really glad Harry cherishes him the way he deserves to be. Popularity's a bitch, but at least he has people who would stand by him no matter what.

Also, I always have my man, Dumbledore, to thank for for always coming to the rescue. Man's leadership and manipulation skills are insane. He's moving all of them like chess pieces. I hope we get to see his perspective and behind the scenes one day. I'd love to write a long review about this book someday. There's a lot to point out, starting with the symbolic death of Cedric — though I so badly wanted him to live. That death was insane 😭, and dare I say, disrespectful. What do you mean they just killed him like that?! LOL, I was rooting for him throughout the book, and if I didn't get spoiled, my world would've stopped at the page he died. That's how AMAZING he is of a character.

And, oh, I just knows it's only gonna get worse from here.

Title: A Tale of Two Cities
Hands-On Machine Learning with Scikit-Learn, Keras & TensorFlow [2nd Edition]
The Psychology of Money: Timeless Lessons on Wealth, Greed, and Happiness
Review:
This reminds me of 1984. Because both books left me with an indescribable paradox of being empty yet overwhelmed with information. Absolutely no questions, I will be keeping this book by my bed to open it every now and then — a privilege I have yet to grant to other books.

First of all, I want to say that this was a very intuitive read. It's my first time to read a physical self-help book chronologically without skipping any paragraph. This can mainly be attributed to the fact that I am very interested in psychology and lately, economics, but Morgan truly did make it easy for me to follow. I was enjoying myself so much that I sometimes didn't realize he changed subjects abruptly or made some crazy leaps.

Second, a lot of points in this book are actually applicable in other aspects of life, especially if you use analogies and get creative with correlations. It's a very nice strength. There's a variety of lessons throughout the chapters: from the proper way of handling your own money to the global movement of the economy. lt's astonishing to read, realize, and understand the extent of how truly connected the world is. That one mere action could lead to a domino of effects. 

Third, I've been wanting to save and invest for years now, but I neither had the opportunities nor the right mindset to start investing and maintain those savings. So my allowances went to purchases for my wants — though I've invested a significant amount of them for the last two years to self-discovery and self-growth. I do regret not saving and investing earlier, but I have to admit that the prices I paid to my soul-searching journey is what allowed me to truly grasp the depth of this book. Therefore, I can now fully unlock and proceea with this new stage of my life. Self-help books, after all, hit better if you know yourself best. 

Fourth, this book made me realize that books that give practical tips and advices to solve problems do not benefit me. I've browsed through HUNDREDS over the years, yet all I felt was dragging myself through the mud. But this one made me float. No joke. I guess, my one takeaway from this is, I should find books that provide an insight on the underlying issues instead of only fixing what's on the surface. I'm a fairly directional person, so all I need to know are rules of the game. From there, I can make my own path. I have a lot of fiction books in my shelves for now (since I bought the entire series of Harry Potter and Game of Thrones lol), so by 97% of this book's influence, I will now be redirecting myself to non-fiction books to cover up the knowledge gaps I have recently found within myself. It will also serve as preparation for when classes start again :P.

Title: Never Split the Difference: Negotiating as If Your Life Depended on It
Little Women
Some People Need Killing: A Memoir of Murder in My Country
White Nights
Review:
Fyodor has been recommended all over my Tiktok and Instagram feed. They said he was a guy who could touch the deepest parts of your soul — and sure enough, he did with mine as well. This was my first book of him and I went through it quickly in two days. There was a lot of fancy vocabulary; you could tell that he wanted to draw you into the emotional world of the book and forget about reality for a while. Heck, it's so effective that I found myself leaving rationality when I grew angry at Nastenka for the way he treated my boi. But alas, who would leave a first love over a man you've just known for four nights?

Contrary to others, I don't think this was romantic in any sense. Maybe Nastenka got her happy ending. But the man needed to touch some grass and not fall in love with the slightest touch of affection. It was quite an adventure walking through his mind and reading his thoughts, but in real life, it's understandable why his social life isn't active.

I'll read Crime and Punishment next. That'll surely be fun. Fyodor's insane words would have you Googling every page, but it's also insane in how much my heart enjoyed it even though I found the plot inherently boring.

Title: Essentialism: The Disciplined Pursuit of Less
The Unfair Advantage: How You Already Have What It Takes to Succeed
Review:
"What do you want for your legacy?"

To be honest, this was undercooked. It could be refined further to add more value, particularly if Ash and Hasan can broaden the concepts further. But I guess they wanted to specialize it for startups. I can see the thought process behind that as enterpreneurs are usually the ones seeking for leverages to exploit, therefore making them the ideal target market. As someone who never truly saw herself in the world of business, there was still quite a lot of insights I could take away from this book and apply to my life in general. 

Things to take away from this:

"Every disadvantage can have a corresponding advantage."

"The pressure of the idea that anyone can be anything if they just try hard enough imbues us all with too much guilt for not achieving what they have."

"Unfair advantages don't just add together, they multiply together."

🔸 Accumulative Advantage

"Speed is absolutely critical to survival."

"All of life is fluid."

"You have to have two opposing viewpoints in your mind without feeling the need to fully reconcile them, and tyen deploy the right persoective when needed."

🔸 dream in that direction

"Learn how to fail."

"You need a bit of naive optimism."

"If you want more luck, take more chances. Be more active."

🔸 observant, proactive, and initiating

"It's not enough to be in the right place at the right time; you also have to be in the right state of mind for it."

"Sometimes, success that comes too early can stunt your growth."

"Correlation is not causation."

🔸 the power of a last name

"Status is your perceived credibility, your perceived ability to add value."

"Difference gives you insights."

"If you do have a form of status, be sure to highlight it when necessary."

"Giving up time in order to reap the benefits of experience and wisdom seems like a small sacrifice."

Title: Psych 101: Psychology Facts, Basics, Statistics, Tests, and More!
Review:
I started this book last year, and it took me a while to get through it because it's a reference book; so it would fulfill its purpose even more if I were to understand it properly instead of rushing through it. Psychology has been a long-time interest of mine, and I'm glad to have been given a concise and clear overview of the subject matter by this book. It offers a little history on the psychologists, the resulting impact of the theories, along with their significance.

Psych 101 truly did enhance my understanding of people and the possible implications of thoughts and behaviors. The one thing I love about psychology is it's not only something that you can read off a book and call it interesting — you can use it in your everyday life. What interested me the most was Louis and Howard's definitions of intelligence. There was a lot I wanted to decode from there.

It struck me the most that no theory is definite in this field. In the same way that some theories feel more intuitive to me, psychologists are also constantly creating new research to prove or disprove one another. This isn't the whole of psychology, yet the book has continuously shown how so many theories were born just to prove Freud wrong (lol, the guy's still pissing off people even when he's six feet under). No wonder he's hailed as the father of modern psychology. However, this also made me realize the potential of research in psychology. There's so much more to uncover because of how diverse and tricky our minds is.

I'll be keeping this book and going back to it from time to time. But generally, I've already taken note of what I need the most. Encyclopedias can provide more information, but this book was short and sweet; perfect if you just want an overview or just getting interested in the topic.

Moods: challenging, dark, informative, mysterious, reflective, sad, tense
Review:
What a mind fuck this book has been. It helped me flesh out my annotation system because life is too long to trust your memory. 1984 sucked my soul out of me and gave me a whole new soul simultaneously. I'll definitely be re-reading it at some point in the future and using it as a reference.

The book was a consuming read — so much so that I had to take a few days to breathe and remind myself that I wasn't in it. The early chapters had me in chill mode, but when I got to chapter nine, I was underlining and tabbing left and right. I enjoyed the world-building so much and terrifyingly, how the elaboration slowly made the contradictions make sense. Some parts of it weren't just applicable in the world of the book or in Oceania in particular — it was relevant to modern society and history. There were always sprinkled bits of truth here and there. Ultimately, the pattern recognition sold me.

Once I got into part three, it was almost impossible to put down. I had to finish it immediately. For me, the most tedious but most important and indeed heaviest parts of the book were the chapters that narrated Winston's torture. It was certainly very hard to go through but a lot were to be learned, and I don't think I can effectively process all of it at the young age of twenty. O'Brien was a filthy, twisted, complex, and outstanding character. He's a villain that is truly a villain, yet you gotta hand it to him for the role the played. I've read tons of Reddit and Goodreads discussions, but I don't know who and what he is.

But I guess that's the selling point of the book: ambiguity. You might as well lose your mind trying to answer all of your questions at once. A lot of readers' theories make sense, especially regarding Julia. How she was possibly raped, had a child, had a lobotomy, and was watching as Winston betrayed her. It made so much sense from the subtle clues Orwell placed in the book. I was always suspicious that O'Brien lied about Julia betraying him first, but at the same time, I wouldn't be surprised if it turns out differently. I also think Mr. Charrington was replaced by a member of the Thought Police, that it wasn't him who gave Winston up.

As for Julia and Winston's relationship, I never once thought of it as a love story. In a free world, they probably wouldn't have bat each other an eye. They wouldn't have worked out. INFJ x ESTP rarely do due to their misaligned values, priorities, world views, and principles. It was more of a companionship born out of loneliness, pent-up desires, and rebellion. Theirs was never a romance. But I do acknowledge the fact that they were loyal to one another in a sense that they tried to hold on as much as possible to their relationship even in the face of their survival instincts.

I'm so glad it was written from Winston's point of view. Any other character would probably have reduced the quality of his book. I loved accompanying his mind in its hyperfixation of the abstracts, possibilities, and ponderings of the real past. It's impressive how he survived for thirty-nine years with so much deception, but one taste of hope made him greedy for more and ultimately led to his downfall. Had he proceeded with his life as it is, I think he would've lived longer. But alas, forty was his destiny of death.

I also found it very amusing how the place where it all started was ultimately where it all ended. Mr. Charrington's shop where he bought that diary. Maybe it was a ploy by the Thought Police, maybe it was when they truly began to observe him strictly. Who knows? Either way, he was damned the moment O'Brien kept an eye on him. "We shall meet in the place where there is no darkness." Damn, what a double meaning. So good yet so bad 😆.

The ending gave me a good laugh, maybe because it was such a relief to finally be done with all of the absurdity. It was a fitting end, nonetheless. Dystopian stories typically end with a successful revolution, but the fact that this book showed how one individual in a huge system was given a tiny hope then quickly stripped of it was more chilling than it would be if it were shown in a hollistic perspective. All the while, it gives you no escape with that sense of helplessness and tramples on every possibility of happiness. Orwell's 1984 was definitely refreshing, the first I've read of its kind, and I understand perfectly why it's a classic.
Content Warnings: Graphic: Physical abuse, Torture, Gaslighting, War;

Title: Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows
Moods: adventurous, challenging, dark, emotional, funny, hopeful, informative, mysterious, reflective, sad, tense
Review:
I knew I had too much to say about this book; but when I finished reading it, the words got sucked out of me like a vacuum. It was that good. The Half-blood Prince only took me five days to finish while this took four with 70% of it being finished in one night. It was that gripping. I couldn't sleep without knowing how this would all end. I had to turn the next page. 

Harry Potter was the first series I bought and read. Safe to say, all the money I spent felt cheap. At first, I had a little regret that this couldn't be a part of my childhood like many others. I was already 20 years old when I first held the The Philosopher's Stone in my hands. But that regret quickly dissapated because I felt like a kid reading through it all anyway, and as I turned the pages, I felt like I grew up with them no matter how old I was already.

It feels good to say goodbye to this series, but at the same time, it's one of those times when you just wanna live in its world forever. Harry Potter felt like a home within pages. When I can finally afford it, I'll buy the MinaLima editions. I just hope by then, they'll have finished until The Deathly Hallows. 

The reason I adore this series despite it not being an intellectual venture like most of my books is because this series taught me to be human. As an ENTJ, the narrative of an ISFP helped me understand how lies don't necessarily carry malice and how truth can carry it instead, how someone could have completely different values from you but still be your best friend, how acceptance can triumph over understanding to provide an amicable result, and how words could mean not literally but utilized as a means to an end.

Now, going from the start, I honestly thought the seven Potters was a brilliant idea. Maybe I'm just happy with the variety, but it was a good plan to confuse the Death Eaters and buy time until the real Harry gets to safety (at the cost of the others'). It was also heartwarming for Harry to ride Sirius' bike with Hagrid for the last time. There's something so symbolic about it. It's like Sirius, even beyond his grave, is still protecting Harry with his everything.

Meanwhile, the death of Hedwig and Dobby was unexpected. I screamed when Dobby died and wished it wasn't true. He was too good to end up like that 🥲. Hedwig's death left me in denial; how could J.K. Rowling just kill the purest creature of this series?! She was Harry's solace! Mad-Eye was kinda eh, but I momentarily got frozen when I read it; glad that his eye was buried by Harry.

Lol, Rita Skeeter won't even leave a dead man in peace. Hermione's imprisonment taught her nothing. Won't do shit for my adoration towards Dumbledore though, he's still my favorite character in the series. Any character who is ridiculously powerful and wicked good at manipulation but still chose to remain kind is automatically a favorite of mine. 

Now, onto the finding of the hocruxes, there have been hints all across the books of how narrow-minded and rigid Hermione is, but in this book, they laid it out so clearly. Girl's thinking is textbook-constrained, any thinking outside of the box is considered delusion to her. But glad Harry is different and is able to form his own opinions. Yet people wonder why they didn't end up together, lol. I don't even fucking know why she and Ron ended up together. Personally, Viktor was a better match. I don't know where Ron and Hermione complement each other except that they've had feelings for each other from Book 4, they've just never been brave enough to tell each other.

I enjoyed the subplot of goblins. It was good. Being able to still include world-building even by the end was a nice element of the series. And in terms of that, I couldn't believe Umbridge was still relevant after the Order of the Phoenix AHAHHAHAHAH. That was a surprise.

As spoiled as I was with the Harry Potter franchise (due to social media), Snape was indeed the biggest plot twist, possibly one of the biggest plot twist I've ever encountered.

However, contrary to the popular choice, I didn't end up loving Snape's character. When I read his backstory, my feelings about him shifted from hate to neutral with 10% more hate. Lol, he wasn't just evil "for Harry", he really was evil. That guy was so unnecessarily cruel to Harry, Neville, and the other students. 

That being said, Snape's loyalty to Lily until the end made me reconsider my perspective on love. Because prior to him, I would've thought that love was natural, genuine, kind, free, sincere, and respectful between the two people. Snape made me realize things in a different light. Sure, his way of loving her was not best. His love for her wasn't clean. But at its core, Snape truly did love her — in spite of not having those feelings returned. That is something that cannot be invalidated. His death was pathetic though, I wish the author allowed him to put up much of a fight. Who cares if it's Lord Voldemort?! At least let him struggle for a bit. Come on, Harry's gone against him multiple times and emerged victorious every time. I guess that's the power of plot armor.

The battle of hogwarts was INSANE. It's a shame the movie couldn't fully portray it. It was so much more magnificent and wild in my mind. It's like everything from Book 1 to Book 7 came together, it was a really nice synthesis!! I could feel all the pent-up history, desires, and rage exploding at once. I think every specie was present there, and I really love the fact that Percy wasn't forgotten and given his redemption arc. It was nice to have all of the Weasley family present for a moment. But I guess J.K. Rowling thought that the return of one Weasley should be paired with the life of another Weasley. 

I ended up loving the character development of Dursley. Glad they gave him that one. It would've been nice if he was a part of the ending, but I guess that's not the focus of the series.

Overall, the way things ended didn't really have that much of an impact on me, but at least it's a happy ending. That was very on brand of Harry Potter.

Title: The Obstacle Is the Way: The Timeless Art of Turning Trials Into Triumph
Beware the Villainess!, Vol. 1
Review:
This is what happens when a 21st-century feminist reincarnates into the body of an 18th-century noblewoman. Melissa is a well-written character. Unlike typical villainesses who are crafted solely for revenge (a cliché I still adore), she’s multidimensional and compelling in her own right.

Her character development is also handled beautifully. As the story progresses, Melissa becomes more self-aware — recognizing her flaws, limitations, and shortcomings — and actively works to improve her circumstances. For example, realizing Jack’s overwhelming physical strength, she takes up swordsmanship and eventually wields the most ridiculously powerful sword in their world.

And while the story clearly favors her, it doesn’t coddle her. Crisis after crisis is thrown at Melissa, but the pacing is done well: tense and engaging, without becoming suffocating.

What makes Melissa stand out even more is her social awareness. She’s unafraid to call out patriarchy and misogyny, and her sharp criticism gives her the confidence and clarity to confront the four protagonists. She’s particularly immune to Ian’s manipulative behavior (seriously, someone please hire that man into an acting agency; might as well make him useful for once).

Now, I’m taking off one star for one reason: Nine.

Compared to everyone else, even Yona with her hilariously unwavering greed, Nine is bland. He irritates me. Maybe it’s because he follows that tired "soft but secretly possessive" trope for male leads. Yes, he’s cute, but his character is undercooked. Worse, Melissa, who is capable of forming complex plans, defeating serious threats, and navigating crisis after crisis without relying on plot armor, suddenly becomes oblivious to his obvious romantic advances? Her intelligence vanishes the moment he enters the scene. It’s honestly a joke.

Despite that, I really loved Melissa’s expressions throughout the series. Her ability to creatively maneuver situations or confront them head-on is magnetic. She is so hot. I simped for her every chapter. No doubt, she’s the hottest female manhwa character I’ve ever come across.

Also? Melissa and Yona’s dynamic is everything. Sure, it’s presented as a transactional relationship driven by Yona’s love of money, but I’d like to think Yona genuinely cares for Melissa. Her loyalty is unwavering, matched only by Nine.

And then there’s the heroine: Yuri. Adorable. I can’t wait to see her character fleshed out further. I loved how she preferred Melissa over the ML—especially during the night market scene. Ian was clearly the third wheel. Peak comedy.

Also? I ship Melissa and Yuri. Hard. It would be amazing if they were endgame 🙈. But of course… we all know it's going to be Nine.

Title: Harry Potter and the Chamber of Secrets
Moods: adventurous, emotional, funny, hopeful, inspiring, mysterious, relaxing, sad, tense
Review: Hmm, I'll give this a 2.5 stars out of my love for the Weasley family. The only thing I enjoyed in this book was the first quarter. It felt good to read Harry and Ron flying the car to Hogwarts, such a shame that so many of its details were skipped in the movie. Otherwise, it felt slow and just... not that interesting at all even tho it is technically interesting and gripping. IDK maybe I'm just getting used to the Harry Potter pattern already.

Title: She Who Became the Sun
Review:
Damn, I thought it was only men that were great at the start but eventually became disappointing, but this book managed to replicate the same behavior. Honestly, that caught me so off guard better than any of this book's plot twist did. I was so invested from the description and first few chapters that I even color-themed my tabs according to the book cover and added sticky notes to a book for the first time. I was thoroughly enjoying the experience until it eventually became bland and somehow I was able to accurately predict the ending.

Ugh, I can't hate this book enough. I'd rather spend my time whipping out paragraphs appreciating an author's good writing. I kept hoping that it wouldn't turn out the way I expected it to be, but as I kept reading, my impatience got the better of me and I skipped to the ending. Turns out, it all happened as I predicted right down to the last detail. Super generic. Wasted money to buy the book *sighs*. 

I do love Esen and Ouyang's relationship tho.

Title: Effortless: Make It Easy to Do What Matters
How the World Works: Mathematics: From creating the pyramids to exploring infinity
The Art of Spending Money: Simple Choices for a Richer Life
Review:
I picked up The Art of Spending a few days after seeing it on Shopee, motivated by how deeply Morgan Housel’s The Psychology of Money changed the way I think about my finances. That book pushed me to finally get my financial life in order — I built savings, created a simple budgeting system, understood my financial behaviors better, and started making decisions with my future self in mind. Naturally, the next question became: How do I spend money well? So when I found this book, I knew I wanted to read it as soon as possible.

Like many reviewers, I agree that this book isn’t as tightly structured or as powerfully organized as his previous one, and yes, some of the ideas may feel like common sense. But I also think this is the kind of book that needs time to sit with you. It feels like something I’ll want to revisit later, when I’ve lived a little more and my relationship with money has evolved further.

One of the concepts I really appreciated was his spectrum of financial dependence to independence. It offers a helpful framework for understanding where I am now and where I hope to be in the future.

And then there’s the line that struck me the hardest—perhaps the most dystopian yet painfully true idea in the book:  

“What actually brings happiness is the contrast between what you have now and whatever you were just experiencing.”

This really made me think. It reframes happiness not as something permanent we eventually arrive at, but as something contextual and relative. It isn’t about endlessly accumulating more or finally reaching a financial milestone; it’s about contrast, movement, and perspective. After struggle, stability feels euphoric. After deprivation, even small comfort feels luxurious. But once those things become normal, the emotional payoff fades and we instinctively look for the next contrast. It’s unsettling because it suggests we may never be fully satisfied.

I also appreciated that Housel didn’t resort to the oversimplified cliché of “money doesn’t buy happiness.” Instead, he acknowledges the complicated truth: money matters. In modern society, it functions almost like air — essential, often invisible until you don’t have enough of it. Dismissing its importance often comes from a place of comfort. At the same time, money isn’t the ultimate meaning of life; it isn’t inherently valuable, but it can be wielded to pursue things that are.

Overall, while The Art of Spending may feel less structured than The Psychology of Money, it still offers ideas worth thinking about — ideas that linger, challenge you, and quietly reshape how you view happiness, value, and what “enough” really means.

Title: Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone
Moods: adventurous, emotional, hopeful, inspiring, lighthearted, relaxing
Review: This was a fun read. I bought the entire series to binge because I thought Harry Potter would give my heart a break from all the heart-wrenching books I had in ny reading diet lately. Surely enough, it did. I ought to add more children's book to my tbr list these days. I loved Ron's sassiness and Harry's school days were very fun to go along with. Hermione was initially insufferable, but her character development was wonderful. It was heartwarming to see the Weasleys accept Harry as one of their own. I won't make this review much longer because I plan to make the real review after finishing the series.

Title: Babel
Moods: challenging, dark, emotional, reflective, sad, tense
Review:
This book inspired me to create a book blog — which I absolutely plan to start ASAP, but will take time to set up. Some things just move you too much that you have to create something for it.

Babel was one hell of a ride. I have 3,681,048,261 things to say about this book that I don't even know where to start. Suddenly I understand why some YouTubers post up to thirty-minute videos just to analyze a character or the plot of a story. Because I feel the same way about Letty and the systematic oppression theme of Babel. I could totally write essays, especially on the death of Professor Lovell. But alas, time is a beautifully painful constraint.


[❤️] This book took too many of my firsts, which are:  

— It was my first time spending 10 dollars for a book, and even then, that was on discount and I found it very expensive. Going forward, I'm probably expanding my budget for one book up to 20 dollars due to finally recognizing the value of a well-written book despite the price. *Babel* was pretty thick, so my money was worth it and it took me a while to consume it.  

— It was my first time reading a book about languages and it really validated my desire to become a polyglot. It made me realize how vast cultures are in general and why they say learning a new language unlocks a new perspective toward the world.  

— No book has ever challenged and inspired me intellectually the way this book did. *1984* was quite the read, but damn, this was on a whole new level. It was emotionally gripping and so thought-provoking at the same time. Joining Robin in his life at Oxford made me escape yet confront reality at the same time.  

— I've never wished for the death of a character I hated so much to be undone the way I did with Lovell. Don't get me wrong, he had it coming and Robin would probably tread into a fate much worse than death if he hadn't struck first, but I did wish it didn't end up that way because everything went spiraling downwards after it. But it was still beautifully written.


[🩵] These characters left their imprints on me:  

1. This book killed a lot of my favorite characters, but I'll never recover from Ramy's death. He was the best character here. Truly a joy to read and his dialogues were hilarious. I liked him the moment he appeared. The best description about him: "He knew how to make himself an object of fascination while neutralizing himself as a threat."

2. I really thought Griffin would live until the end, tf. He was interestingly written. I understood his paranoia, agreed with a lot of his beliefs, and ultimately realized why he could never fully trust Robin until the circumstances demanded it. Out of everyone, he was the most worthy to live. But I guess he lived on through Robin? And his ideas were brought to fruition, though it was a little late. Everyone should've listened to him earlier.  

3. Evie. Sweet, innocent, dear Evie — or was she? This POS haunts the plot beautifully and goddamn I wish there was an entire book just for her. She's a villain that, though doesn't have much of a presence, you can't help but respect. If she would've lived on, she would've been a true menace — even more than Playfair's numerous inventions.  

4. And then there's this woman that did nothing but piss me off 70% of the book until I got to her POV and began to appreciate the symbolism of her character. Sadly, Letty's existence is realistic, which is probably why it pisses a lot of people off so much. There was no saving her; she didn't want to be saved. The system benefitted her, so she doesn't see a need to question the status quo. It's also befitting of her to never be able to understand why Ramy never reciprocated her feelings.


[💛] The following quotes resonated with me:

"The translator dances in shackles."  

"...as if his loyalties were shackles by privilege he had not asked for and did not choose to receive."  

"He hoped. He hoped until hope became its own form of torture."  

"But they could not touch that abyss of grief. It was too early yet to give it a name, to shape and tame it with words."  

"If they're going to tell stories about you, use it to your advantage."  

"But what do we know of thought and feeling except as expressed through language?"  

"...to justify a vice with virtue."


[💜] This interaction was my highlight:

"You mean witchcraft," said Ramy. "That's not natural."

Letty snorted. "You need to meet more women."
Content Warnings: Graphic: Death, Emotional abuse, Infidelity, Misogyny, Physical abuse, Racial slurs, Sexual assault, Torture, Violence, Grief, Gaslighting, Sexual harassment, Colonisation;

Title: The 80/20 Principle, Expanded and Updated: The Secret to Achieving More with Less
Review:
Balance is the perfect imbalance, that's the central theme of this book. Honestly, this book is perfect for lazy ambitious people. It talks about how reframing your focus to better things will lead to a more fulfilled life. Because let's face it, not everything is important. The author itself applied the 80/20 principle to his own book. Most of the content is skippable once you get the overall gist of the principle because they're just sets of data and historical events dedicated to proving one point right — that a small number of efforts lead to most results.

Here are some lines in the book that are worth remembering:

"The universe is unbalanced. The world is not linear; cause and effect are rarely linked in an equal way."

"Do not tackle everything with equal effort."

"People who achieve the most are selective as well as determined."

"You are hugely more productive at some things than at others, but dilute the effectiveness of this by doing too many things where your comparative skill is nowhere near as great."

"There are always winners and losers."

"You are more likely to win by rigging the odds in your favor than by striving to improve your performance."

"We don't need time management — we need a time revolution."

"There is no value in doing things you don't enjoy."

Title: Why Machines Learn: The Elegant Math Behind Modern AI
The Complete Book of Chess Strategy: Grandmaster Techniques from A to Z
The War of Art: Break Through the Blocks & Win Your Inner Creative Battles
The Practicing Mind: Bringing Discipline and Focus into Your Life
Thinking, Fast and Slow
The 4-Hour Workweek
The Seven Husbands of Evelyn Hugo
Review:
First book of this year and it DID NOT disappoint.

I’m honestly not a fan of romance as a genre. I actively avoid it most of the time. The main reason this book ended up on my TBR was because it’s LGBTQ+, it stayed hyped on social media long after its peak, and the title intrigued the hell out of me — especially after I got spoiled that Evelyn had been in love with a woman all along.

Not gonna lie, the beginning didn’t hook me. It felt bland, and Evelyn irritated me at first because she carried this air of entitlement. But once the biography started, I couldn’t put the book down. I actually regret not reading it as soon as I received the package. I’ve been looking for a book that could completely pull me into its world again (The Secret History is still moving a bit slow for me), and this one did exactly that. For two days straight I was absorbed in it, and even days after finishing, I still found myself thinking about it.

It’s honestly bizarre realizing Evelyn isn’t a real person, that this isn’t actually a biography based on real life.

There wasn’t a specific character I ended up loving the way I have in other books (like Cedric from Harry Potter or Ramy from Babel), but I did appreciate the complexity of Evelyn. I understand her line of thinking because I’m also an ENTJ. I know what it feels like to want power.

One thing I really disliked was the implication that Harry, despite being gay, still had a small part of him that wanted to sleep with Evelyn. That line honestly threw me off. Before his sexuality was revealed, I had actually been rooting for him and Evelyn because he treated her with so much respect. That said, I ended up loving the direction their relationship took. Their platonic soulmate dynamic was beautiful, and it was comforting to see Evelyn find genuine solace in someone despite the emptiness of Hollywood. I also agreed that having a child together made sense, and I was glad he turned out to be the father.

As for Evelyn and Celia, I’ll just say it outright: they were toxic. Yes, the sensual scenes are written well, but lust still feels like a recurring theme throughout their relationship. I don’t think Evelyn ever truly escaped seeing herself as an object of desire. Even in her own narration, there are moments where she admits feeling objectified by Celia.

Their constant on-and-off never felt like yearning or romantic pining to me. It mostly felt like pride getting in the way of communication, wasting years they could have spent together. Every time they reunited, it felt more like mutual neediness than genuine resolution. Their values and principles were often misaligned, and the book never fully explored that — it just skipped ahead to them reconciling. That felt like an important conflict that shouldn’t have been glossed over.

While Celia and Evelyn did have me smiling, there was no romance in this book I wholeheartedly enjoyed and wanted to replicate.

I was also genuinely surprised Evelyn even entertained Max. By that point she was already forty. You’d think she would have learned those lessons by then; that some men are only excellent in the beginning, when they’re trying to acquire you, but are never actually capable of loving you.

As for Monique, she was… unimpressive. But to be fair, anyone would seem unimpressive when Evelyn Hugo dominates the narrative. I did like that she eventually took responsibility for her own life, ended her marriage, and started making decisions for herself. In a way, Evelyn pushed her toward taking those leaps so she wouldn’t reach the end of her life full of regret — something Evelyn herself had plenty of.

I’m also glad the story didn’t go for the predictable ending where Monique tries to save Evelyn with a speech about how precious life is. If someone knows it’s their time to go, let them go. I’ve never understood why people struggle with that idea so much. The twist involving Monique’s father was interesting, but it didn’t impress me nearly as much as the rest of Evelyn’s story.

Overall, it was fascinating to grow up alongside Evelyn — her humble beginnings, her rise to stardom, her love for Celia, her platonic marriage with Harry, their child (though I still think Connor is such a strange name for a girl), and eventually her retirement. Despite everything they went through, I did find it wholesome that Celia spent the last eleven years of her life with Evelyn in a quiet part of Spain, where they could finally love each other freely.

The quote that stuck to me: "I should have apologized and sent roses and stood on top of the Hollywood sign and shouted 'I'm in love with Celia St. James!' and let them crucify me for it."

Title: The Intelligent Investor
Harry Potter and the Prisoner of Azkaban
Moods: adventurous, emotional, funny, hopeful, inspiring, lighthearted, relaxing, tense
Review:
I had a slow start to this book because the second one bore the fuck out of me and I was occupied with our Final Examinations, but Professor Lupin brought me back to life. Honestly, I might prefer this over the Philosopher's Stone just because of the cast, namely: Lupin, Sirius, James, and Ron. Hagrid's classes deserved better. It had so much good potential :((. Fuck you, Malfoy. This is probably the first book where he was snobbed most of the time. He did not have as much "screen time" than he did with the previous ones. 

It was heartwarming to see Harry finally having a relationship with his godfather. I was very happy for him when Sirius proposed that Harry lived with him. But alas, that could not happen 😔 YET. I hope in the future, it will. Sirius sounds like a good uncle that Harry will learn a lot from and have lots of fun with. 

Meanwhile, Lupin. Lupin was my absolute favorite character in this. I don't fucking care that he's as haggard as the books describe him. He's a fine man on my mind (not simping on him, stop those weird side eyes on my direction). He was a COOL guy and absolutely the dark arts teacher they all deserved. How dare Snape rob us of that. Also he was a strong and proportional werewolf in my mind. Not that thin shit on the movies. I would love to learn more about James Potter and his gang in the next books :)).

Oh, and Ron. There's nothing more to say about him. Ron is always adorable throughout all the books. Best bro. I love how he's always by Harry's side as Harry is with his 🥰. He's the character that constantly makes me happy in the series.

Title: Harry Potter and the Order of the Phoenix
Moods: adventurous, dark, emotional, funny, mysterious, tense
Review:
I have so many things to say about this book, but I don’t really feel like writing a super long review, so I’ll just try to condense my thoughts.

Even though the length was kind of intimidating at first, I ended up loving how rich the world-building was. I actually enjoyed taking my time with it. There’s something satisfying about holding a thick book and knowing you’ve read every single word. It made me realize: I really like long books. I want more of them. War and Peace is next on my list (LOL, I hope it doesn’t take me longer than two months).

This was the first Harry Potter book that had me hooked right from the start. Normally, the earlier chapters are more slow and quiet, and then things go crazy toward the end (especially with what happened in The Goblet of Fire). But in this one, I was gripped immediately. Ironically, by the time I reached the end, my attention had already started drifting. It felt like all the best stuff was packed into the middle.

It was nice to see Ron finally getting the appreciation and spotlight he deserved. Although, Ron and Hermione's prefect duties fell halfway through the book, it would've been nice for it to be relevant all throughout. On the other hand, Draco’s honestly a joke at this point. The only reason he’s overhyped is because Tom Felton happens to be conventionally attractive. I have to keep reminding myself he’s just fifteen, of course his attempts at power plays are pathetic. But even so, I can’t ignore how his behavior often serve as symbolic sign of deeper issues; the very mindset that enables and perpetuates broken systems.

I also really enjoyed the addition of new characters, especially Luna and Tonks. Luna, in particular, was such a fun new element, and honestly, her portrayal in the movie was pretty accurate. They nailed her dreamy voice. On the flip side, Umbridge was insane. Like, how is it even possible to hate a side character more than the actual villain? 🤣 Girlie made Snape look kind in comparison. That’s a real achievement. It was also pretty cool seeing all the Defence Against the Dark Arts teachers show up in the same book.

Now, let’s talk about Harry. I’ve seen some people say his outbursts were annoying, but come on; the guy is sixteen, has trauma, and is constantly being left in the dark while literally being hunted by Voldemort. His anger felt so valid to me. Imagine being treated like a fragile glass that could shatter from one touch when you’ve gone through way more than the adults in the series. Imagine being led like a puppet and expected to follow instantly WITHOUT EXPLANATION when you're the most worthy of explanation. Everyone's trying to "protect" him and are doing an absurdly shitty job at it. Even Dumbledore admitted that in the end. So yeah, I’m defending my boy on this one.

As for Sirius, I can’t believe I’m saying this, but I didn’t even feel sad when he died. Same as with Cedric, his death was just thrown out in there and felt disrespectful. Though truthfully, I grew to dislike Sirius in this book, which sucks because I really liked him in Prisoner of Azkaban. His loyalty is admirable, sure, but he honestly just felt like another teenager trapped in an adult’s body.

"You’re less like your father than I thought." Molly was right. Sirius was projecting James onto Harry. Lupin was a better father figure for Harry, but if you ask me, he's just the lesser evil. None of the two saw what was wrong with bullying someone even until their adulthood. 

All that said, I still want to give this book some serious credit. The world-building was incredible, even better than The Goblet of Fire, and there were so many moments that made the reading experience genuinely enjoyable. Honestly, I don’t remember laughing as much in any of the previous books.

Some of my favorite lines:

“You don’t mind if we don’t kiss you, do you, Ron?”

“I suppose Lord Voldemort’s just a warm-up act compared to you three.”

“You’re well out of it, mate. I mean, she’s quite good-looking and all that, but you want someone a bit more cheerful.”

“Extra lessons with Snape — what on earth had he done to deserve this?”

“Women!” Harry muttered angrily.

Title: The Hate U Give
Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince
Moods: adventurous, challenging, dark, emotional, funny, mysterious, sad, tense
Review:
Originally just three stars, but I'll give it more for Dumbledore's death. It was given the respect it deserved. Compared to the prior releases, there wasn't anything I particularly liked in the beginning or middle, but I did enjoy reading it. It did surprise me, however, that Draco couldn't kill in the end. The half-blood prince felt like an irrelevant side quest tho, even tho it's the title. Dumbledore was the only thing I enjoyed and loved in this book. A lot happened, yet when I looked back, there's nothing much to remember.

Still can't get over Fleur's line, "I am good-looking enough for the both of us." Like, slay?!?!?! Damn, the flex to be able to say that. Girlie was cooking.
